title: Lorentzian Polynomials
overall_summary: The paper develops the theory of Lorentzian homogeneous polynomials with nonnegative coefficients and proves that this analytic class is exactly controlled by M-convex supports and one-positive-eigenvalue Hessian conditions. Its most relevant contribution for matroidal/tropical questions is the equivalence between M-convex functions, valuated matroids, and tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over Puiseux series; in particular, the Dressian of a matroid with basis set B is identified, up to sign and closure, with tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials whose support is B. The paper also proves robust closure properties under nonnegative linear substitutions, directional derivatives, polarization/projection, and certain linear operators, giving a toolbox for constructing and transporting valuated matroids. Topologically, strata of Lorentzian polynomials with fixed M-convex support, including fixed matroid-basis support, are nonempty and contractible via coefficient-power homotopies. These results could be useful for the original problem whenever elements of a relative Dressian are encoded by valuated matroids or M-convex functions and one wants to construct maps, lifts, or deformations compatible with matroid minors or flats.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L^d_n denote the paper's closed Lorentzian class of degree d homogeneous polynomials in variables w_1,...,w_n with nonnegative coefficients: for d=0,1 it is the stable class, for d=2 it is the class of quadratic stable polynomials with nonnegative coefficients, and for d>2 it consists of polynomials with M-convex support whose first partial derivatives all lie in L^{d-1}_n. If f is in L^d_n, if i,j are indices in {1,...,n}, and if θ is a nonnegative real number, then (1+θ w_i ∂_j)f is again in L^d_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first checks the support. Lemma 2.8 shows that if f has M-convex support, then applying 1+θ w_i ∂_j preserves M-convexity; the proof of that lemma realizes the support operation by elementary splitting of a variable, restriction to an interval, and elementary aggregation, using the fact from discrete convex analysis that splitting and aggregation preserve M-convexity. It remains to prove that every quadratic derivative is stable. For a multi-index α of weight d-2, write g=(1+θ w_i ∂_j)f. If α_i=0, then ∂^α g=∂^α f+θ w_i ∂^{α+e_j}f, and stability follows from the standard facts that derivatives of stable polynomials are stable and interlace the original polynomial. If α_i>0, then ∂^α g=∂^α f+θ α_i ∂^{α-e_i+e_j}f+θ w_i ∂^{α+e_j}f; the first two derivative terms are controlled by interlacing relations coming from Lemma 2.9, and positive linear combinations preserve stability in the required interlacing cone. The only delicate case is when ∂^{α+e_j}f vanishes; the argument reduces to a cubic, perturbs f by h_s=(1+s w_i ∂_{i_1})f to create the missing mixed derivative, applies the nondegenerate case, and then lets s tend to 0, using stability as a closed condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f(w_1,...,w_n) be in L^d_n, and let A be an n by m matrix with nonnegative real entries. If v=(v_1,...,v_m), then the homogeneous polynomial f(Av) in the variables v_1,...,v_m lies in L^d_m. In words, Lorentzian polynomials are closed under arbitrary nonnegative linear changes of variables.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces an arbitrary nonnegative matrix to three elementary operations: elementary splitting of a variable, dilation of a variable by a scalar θ≥0, and diagonalization/identification of two variables. Splitting is obtained by the limit lim_{k→∞}(1+w_{n+1}∂_n/k)^k f=f(w_1,...,w_{n-1},w_n+w_{n+1}); Proposition 2.7 ensures each finite stage is Lorentzian, while a separate M-convexity check is made before taking the limit. Dilation is immediate for θ>0 and follows at θ=0 because setting a variable to zero preserves M-convexity and the derivative conditions. Diagonalization is treated similarly to splitting by applying lim_{k→∞}(1+w_{n-1}∂_n/k)^k and then setting w_n=0. Since every nonnegative linear substitution is a composition of these operations, the theorem follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If f is in L^d_n and a_1,...,a_n are nonnegative real numbers, then the nonnegative directional derivative Σ_i a_i ∂_i f lies in L^{d-1}_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 2.10 to the polynomial f after adjoining a new variable w_{n+1} via the nonnegative substitution w_i ↦ w_i+a_i w_{n+1}. The resulting polynomial g(w,w_{n+1})=f(w_1+a_1w_{n+1},...,w_n+a_nw_{n+1}) lies in L^d_{n+1}. Its derivative ∂_{n+1}g lies in L^{d-1}_{n+1} by definition of L^d. Finally set w_{n+1}=0 using another nonnegative linear substitution, obtaining Σ_i a_i ∂_i f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a nonzero homogeneous polynomial of degree d≥2 in real variables w_1,...,w_n. If f is strictly Lorentzian, then for every w in the positive orthant R^n_{>0}, the Hessian matrix H_f(w) is nonsingular. If f is Lorentzian, then for every w in R^n_{>0}, H_f(w) has exactly one positive eigenvalue.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof proceeds by induction on d. For d=2 the assertion is part of the definition and the quadratic characterization in Lemma 2.5. For d≥3, Lemma 2.15 gives a kernel identity: if the Hessians of all first derivatives ∂_i f each have exactly one positive eigenvalue at w, then ker H_f(w) is the intersection of the kernels of the Hessians H_{∂_i f}(w). This identity uses Euler's formula for homogeneous functions, the relation (d-2)H_f=Σ_i w_i H_{∂_i f}, and the fact that a matrix with exactly one positive eigenvalue is negative semidefinite on the hyperplane orthogonal to a positive direction. Induction gives nonsingularity for the derivative Hessians in the strict case, so H_f is nonsingular. For the one-positive-eigenvalue assertion, the theorem first handles stable polynomials by showing the quadratic part of f(z+w) is stable and hence has one positive eigenvalue; since the strictly Lorentzian cone is connected by the Nuij-type homotopy and nonsingularity prevents eigenvalues from crossing zero, the signature persists. General Lorentzian polynomials follow by approximation by strictly Lorentzian polynomials.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.17 and Proposition 2.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a homogeneous polynomial of degree d≥2 with nonnegative coefficients. If H_f(w) has exactly one positive eigenvalue for every w in R^n_{>0}, then for all i,j and all w in R^n_{≥0}, one has f(w) ∂_i∂_j f(w) ≤ 2(1-1/d) ∂_i f(w) ∂_j f(w). Consequently every polynomial in L^d_n is c-Rayleigh with c=2(1-1/d), meaning that for every multi-index α, every i,j, and every w in R^n_{≥0}, ∂^α f(w) ∂^{α+e_i+e_j}f(w) ≤ c ∂^{α+e_i}f(w) ∂^{α+e_j}f(w), with the same c valid because 2(1-1/e) increases with the degree e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix w in the positive orthant and write H=H_f(w). Euler's formula gives w^T H w=d(d-1)f(w) and w^T H e_i=(d-1)∂_i f(w). For a real parameter t, restrict H to the plane spanned by w and v_t=e_i+t e_j. Since H has exactly one positive eigenvalue, Cauchy interlacing implies that the determinant of this two-dimensional restriction is nonpositive. Expanding the determinant yields a quadratic inequality in t. A weaker quadratic inequality, retaining only the mixed second derivative term, must hold for all t; requiring its discriminant to be nonpositive gives f ∂_i∂_j f ≤ 2(1-1/d)∂_i f ∂_j f. The Lorentzian c-Rayleigh statement applies this estimate to all derivatives of f, using Theorem 2.16 for the Hessian signature of each nonzero Lorentzian derivative.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a homogeneous polynomial with nonnegative coefficients. If f is c-Rayleigh for some positive real number c, in the sense that all inequalities ∂^α f(w) ∂^{α+e_i+e_j}f(w) ≤ c ∂^{α+e_i}f(w) ∂^{α+e_j}f(w) hold for all multi-indices α, indices i,j, and w in R^n_{≥0}, then the support of f is an M-convex subset of N^n. Here M-convex means that for any α,β in the support and any i with α_i>β_i, there exists j with α_j<β_j such that α-e_i+e_j and β-e_j+e_i are both in the support.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first establishes two structural lemmas for supports of c-Rayleigh polynomials. Lemma 2.22(1) proves interval convexity: if α and β are in the support and α≤γ≤β coordinatewise, then γ is in the support. This is proved by choosing a minimal counterexample, reducing by contractions and deletions, and evaluating the Rayleigh inequality near the origin to force a contradiction between the lowest-order terms. Lemma 2.22(2) proves that if f(0)≠0, then supp(f) is M^natural-convex; this uses interval convexity plus the augmentation criterion of Lemma 2.21 and again argues by minimal counterexample, reducing to a violation of a Rayleigh inequality after differentiating by α-e_i. For homogeneous f, translate by the positive vector 1: g(w)=f(w+1) is c-Rayleigh by Lemma 2.20 and has nonzero constant term. Thus supp(g) is M^natural-convex, so the support of its homogenization is M-convex. The original support of f is obtained by intersecting this homogenized M-convex set with a coordinate hyperplane, and such intersections preserve M-convexity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n and d, the closure in the coefficient topology of the strictly Lorentzian cone °L^d_n is exactly L^d_n. Equivalently, a homogeneous degree d polynomial with nonnegative coefficients is Lorentzian if and only if its support is M-convex and every derivative of order d-2 is a Lorentzian quadratic, i.e. a quadratic form with nonnegative coefficients whose Hessian has at most one positive eigenvalue.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One inclusion is Theorem 2.13: every polynomial in L^d_n is a limit of strictly Lorentzian polynomials. The proof of Theorem 2.13 uses a Nuij-type homotopy: first apply a positive averaging substitution S(θ,f) that sends f toward (w_1+...+w_n)^d while keeping the polynomial in L^d_n by Theorem 2.10; then apply the operator T_n(θ,−)=∏_{i<n}(1+θ w_i∂_n)^d. Lemma 2.12 shows that for θ>0 this produces a strictly Lorentzian polynomial, and as θ→0 one recovers f. For the reverse inclusion, limits of strictly Lorentzian polynomials are limits of c-Rayleigh polynomials by Proposition 2.19, hence are c-Rayleigh; Theorem 2.23 forces their supports to be M-convex. The derivative quadratic conditions are closed under limits, so the limiting polynomial belongs to L^d_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P H^d_n be the projective space of degree d homogeneous polynomials in n variables, and let P L^d_n be the image in P H^d_n of the nonzero Lorentzian polynomials L^d_n. Then P L^d_n is compact and contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Compactness follows because L^d_n is a closed cone by Theorem 2.25, and projectivizing may be identified with intersecting the cone with the Euclidean unit sphere in coefficient space. Contractibility is obtained from the explicit deformation constructed in the proof of Theorem 2.13: nonnegative linear substitutions move any Lorentzian polynomial toward the symmetric polynomial (w_1+...+w_n)^d, and the Nuij operator T_n preserves the Lorentzian cone. After projectivization this gives a deformation retraction to a point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.30
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a homogeneous polynomial f with nonnegative coefficients, the following are equivalent: (1) f is completely log-concave, meaning that for every finite product of nonnegative directional derivatives D_1...D_m f, the result is either zero or has log concave on the positive orthant; (2) f is strongly log-concave, meaning that every ordinary partial derivative ∂^α f is either zero or log-concave on the positive orthant; (3) f is Lorentzian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication complete log-concave implies strong log-concave is immediate. For strong log-concavity, Proposition 2.33 identifies log-concavity of a nonzero homogeneous polynomial with the property that its Hessian has exactly one positive eigenvalue on the positive orthant. Applying Proposition 2.17 to all derivatives makes f c-Rayleigh, and then Theorem 2.23 gives M-convexity of the support; Theorem 2.25 then yields Lorentzianity. Conversely, if f is Lorentzian, Theorem 2.16 and Proposition 2.33 give log-concavity of f and of all Lorentzian derivatives. Complete log-concavity follows because Corollary 2.11 shows that every nonnegative directional derivative of a Lorentzian polynomial is again Lorentzian, so arbitrary products of such derivatives retain the same Hessian/log-concavity property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix κ=(κ_1,...,κ_n) in N^n. Let R_κ[w] be the space of polynomials of degree at most κ_i in each variable w_i, and let R^a_κ[w_{ij}] be the multi-affine polynomial ring in variables w_{ij} for 1≤i≤n and 1≤j≤κ_i. The projection Π^↓_κ substitutes every w_{ij} by w_i. The polarization Π^↑_κ sends w^α to the normalized product over i of the elementary symmetric polynomial of degree α_i in w_{i1},...,w_{iκ_i}, divided by ∏_i binom(κ_i,α_i). Both Π^↓_κ and Π^↑_κ preserve the Lorentzian property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Projection is just a nonnegative linear substitution, so Theorem 2.10 applies. For polarization, it suffices by density to treat strictly Lorentzian f. The support of Π^↑_κ f is the full Boolean slice of the correct degree, hence M-convex. For any derivative of order d-2 of the polarized polynomial, after renaming variables it is a positive scalar times the polarization of the corresponding quadratic derivative ∂^α f. Quadratic derivatives of f are stable by strict Lorentzianity, and polarization preserves stability by the Borcea-Brändén polarization theorem. Thus all required quadratic derivatives are stable, so Theorem 2.25 gives Lorentzianity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let κ in N^n and γ in N^m. Let T:R_κ[w_1,...,w_n]→R_γ[w_1,...,w_m] be a homogeneous linear operator of degree ℓ, meaning T(w^α) is either zero or homogeneous of degree |α|+ℓ. Define its symbol by sym_T(w,u)=Σ_{0≤α≤κ} binom(κ,α) T(w^α) u^{κ-α}, a homogeneous polynomial in the output variables w and auxiliary input variables u. If sym_T is Lorentzian of degree |κ|+ℓ and f is Lorentzian of degree d with f in R_κ[w], then T(f) is Lorentzian of degree d+ℓ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first polarizes both the operator and the input. The symbol of the polarized operator is the polarization of sym_T, hence is Lorentzian by Proposition 3.1. Thus one reduces to the multi-affine case κ=(1,...,1) and γ=(1,...,1). In the multi-affine case, write sym_T(w,u)f(v)=Σ_{S⊂[n]}T(w^S)u^{[n]\S}f(v), which is Lorentzian because products of Lorentzian polynomials are Lorentzian. Apply, for each i, a two-variable operator T_{u_i,v_i} from Lemma 3.3 that merges the auxiliary variables u_i and v_i in a way that preserves Lorentzianity. After all n such operators, one obtains Σ_S T(w^S)(∂_S f)(v), still Lorentzian. Setting all v variables to zero is a nonnegative linear specialization and yields exactly T(f), so T(f) is Lorentzian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4 and Corollaries 3.5, 3.7, 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If T is a homogeneous linear operator that preserves stable polynomials and also preserves polynomials with nonnegative coefficients, then T preserves Lorentzian polynomials. Consequences include: the multi-affine part of a Lorentzian polynomial is Lorentzian; the normalization operator N sending w^α to w^α/α! preserves Lorentzian polynomials; and if N(f) and N(g) are Lorentzian, then N(fg) is Lorentzian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 3.4 uses the Borcea-Brändén classification of stability-preserving linear operators. Such an operator either has rank at most two with stable image, or its symbol sym_T(w,u) or sym_T(w,−u) is stable. In the rank-at-most-two case, the output is stable with nonnegative coefficients and hence Lorentzian by Proposition 2.2. In the symbol cases, nonnegativity of coefficients and stability imply that the relevant symbol is Lorentzian, and Theorem 3.2 applies. The multi-affine-part operator is known to preserve stability and nonnegative coefficients, so it preserves Lorentzianity. The normalization operator is treated by Theorem 3.2 directly: its symbol factors into bivariate polynomials whose coefficient sequences 1/k! are log-concave in the required normalized sense, so the symbol is Lorentzian. The convolution statement N(fg) is proved by writing multiplication by g after normalization as a linear operator and verifying, via a second symbol computation and Theorem 3.2, that its symbol is Lorentzian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10 and Lemma 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a nonempty subset J of N^n, let f_J=Σ_{α in J} w^α/α! be its exponential generating function. The following are equivalent: J is M-convex; f_J is Lorentzian; f_J is homogeneous c-Rayleigh for some c>0; and there exists some Lorentzian, or homogeneous c-Rayleigh, polynomial whose support is J. If J is contained in the Boolean cube {0,1}^n, these conditions are also equivalent to J being the set of bases of a matroid on [n]. The key quadratic input, Lemma 3.11, says that for a symmetric {0,1}-matrix A, the quadratic polynomial w^T A w is Lorentzian if and only if its support is M-convex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications from Lorentzian to Rayleigh to M-convex use Proposition 2.19 and Theorem 2.23. To prove that an M-convex J gives a Lorentzian f_J, one uses Theorem 2.25 and checks all degree-two derivatives. Any derivative ∂^α f_J has support obtained from J by translation and contraction, hence still M-convex; thus the problem reduces to Lemma 3.11 for quadratic {0,1}-coefficient forms. For Lemma 3.11, after deleting zero columns, let S be the set of indices i for which 2e_i is in the support. The exchange axiom forces e_i+e_j to be in the support for all i in S and all j. On the remaining indices, the support is the basis set of a rank-two matroid without loops, so it decomposes into parallel classes S_1,...,S_k. The quadratic form can then be written as (Σ_j w_j)^2−Σ_{r=1}^k(Σ_{j in S_r}w_j)^2, which visibly has at most one positive eigenvalue. The Boolean-cube assertion is the standard equivalence between M-convex subsets of a fixed Boolean slice and matroid basis sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.25 and Remark 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f=Σ_{α} c_α w^α/α! be a Lorentzian homogeneous polynomial with nonnegative normalized coefficients. For 0≤p≤1, define R_p(f)=Σ_{α in supp(f)} c_α^p w^α/α!, with the convention that R_0(f)=f_{supp(f)}. Then R_p(f) is Lorentzian for every p in [0,1]. Consequently, for every nonempty M-convex set J, the projectivized stratum of Lorentzian polynomials with support exactly J is contractible. In the multi-affine case, for every rank d matroid with basis set B⊂[n choose d], the projective stratum P L_B of Lorentzian multi-affine polynomials with support B is nonempty and contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of Proposition 3.25 reduces by Theorem 2.25 and Theorem 2.10 to the case of a positive-coefficient quadratic form. Lemma 3.24 is the key linear algebra statement: if a positive symmetric matrix (a_{ij}) has exactly one positive eigenvalue, then its entrywise p-th power (a_{ij}^p) also has exactly one positive eigenvalue for 0≤p≤1. This uses Perron-Frobenius: after scaling by the positive Perron eigenvector, the matrix is conditionally negative definite, and a theorem on powers of conditionally negative definite kernels preserves this property. Applying the lemma to every quadratic derivative gives Lorentzianity of R_p(f). The contractibility statement follows because p↦R_p(f) continuously deforms every polynomial in the fixed-support stratum to the canonical generating function f_J without changing support for p>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a function d on two-element subsets of [n], define the symmetric matrix H_q(d) by diagonal entries 0 and off-diagonal entries q^{d(i,j)}. The following are equivalent: H_q(d) has exactly one positive eigenvalue for every q≥1; and d is a tree distance, meaning d(i,j) is the sum of edge weights along the unique path between leaves i and j in some phylogenetic tree with n labelled leaves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the implication from the spectral condition to tree distance, one first reduces to rational d and observes that w^T H_q(d)w is stable for every q≥1. By quantifier elimination over real closed fields, the Puiseux polynomial Σ_{i<j} t^{-d(i,j)}w_iw_j is stable. Brändén's tropicalization theorem for stable polynomials implies that −d is M-convex on the rank-two Boolean slice, equivalently d lies in the rank-two Dressian Dr(2,n), which equals the space T_n of phylogenetic tree distances. Conversely, if d is an ultrametric, then q^d is again an ultrametric for q≥1; by Timan-Vestfrid and Schoenberg, the associated squared distance matrix is conditionally negative semidefinite, hence H_q(d) has at most one positive eigenvalue, and Perron-Frobenius gives exactly one when appropriate. A general tree distance differs from an ultrametric by an element of the linearity space of T_n, which corresponds to congruence by a positive diagonal matrix and preserves inertia.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ν:∆^d_n→R∪{∞} be a function on the degree d discrete simplex, with effective domain dom(ν)={α:ν(α)<∞}. Define f^ν_q(w)=Σ_{α in dom(ν)} q^{ν(α)} w^α/α! and g^ν_q(w)=Σ_{α in dom(ν)} binom(δ,α) q^{ν(α)} w^α, where δ=(d,...,d) and binom(δ,α)=∏_i binom(d,α_i). Then the following are equivalent: ν is M-convex in the discrete convex analysis sense, i.e. it satisfies the symmetric exchange inequality ν(α)+ν(β)≥ν(α−e_i+e_j)+ν(β−e_j+e_i) for suitable j whenever α_i>β_i; f^ν_q is Lorentzian for every 0<q≤1; and g^ν_q is Lorentzian for every 0<q≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from M-convexity to Lorentzianity of g^ν_q is proved in stages. First assume dom(ν) is the full Boolean slice [n choose d]. Then every quadratic derivative corresponds to a rank-two valuated matroid, and Lemma 3.21 supplies the one-positive-eigenvalue condition for all required quadratic forms. Second, when dom(ν)=∆^d_n, polarize ν to a function Π^↑ν on a Boolean slice; Lemma 3.26 preserves M-convexity, the Boolean case applies, and Proposition 3.1 projects Lorentzianity back. Third, for arbitrary M-convex effective domain and q=1, Theorem 3.10 handles the support. For arbitrary domain and q<1, Lemma 3.27 approximates ν by finite-valued M-convex functions ν_k with full domain and ν_k→ν; closedness of the Lorentzian cone gives the result. To pass from g^ν_q to f^ν_q, apply the already proved result to ν/p and then use Proposition 3.25: R_p(g^{ν/p}_q) is Lorentzian and tends to f^ν_q as p→0. The reverse implications use quantifier elimination: if f^ν_q or g^ν_q is Lorentzian for all small q, then the corresponding Puiseux polynomial is Lorentzian over K and has tropicalization ν; Theorem 3.20 then forces ν to be M-convex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f=Σ_{α in ∆^d_n} c_α w^α/α! be a homogeneous polynomial with nonnegative coefficients. Define ν_f(α)=log(c_α), with ν_f(α)=−∞ when c_α=0. If ν_f is M-concave, equivalently −ν_f is M-convex, then f is Lorentzian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3.14 to the M-convex function −ν_f. Taking q=e^{-1} gives coefficients q^{−ν_f(α)}=e^{ν_f(α)}=c_α in the normalized form, so f is exactly the corresponding polynomial f^{−ν_f}_{e^{-1}} and is Lorentzian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.26 and Lemma 3.27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ν:∆^d_n→R∪{∞}. Its polarization Π^↑ν is the function on a Boolean slice in variables e_{ij} whose value at a Boolean vector β is ν(φ(β)), where φ(e_{ij})=e_i; its projection Π^↓µ of a function µ on the polarized simplex is the function α↦min{µ(β):φ(β)=α}. Lemma 3.26 states that polarization and projection preserve M-convexity. Lemma 3.27 states that every M-convex ν:∆^d_n→R∪{∞} is the pointwise limit of finite-valued M-convex functions ν_k:∆^d_n→R; moreover ν_k can be chosen equal to ν on dom(ν) and strictly increasing outside dom(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3.26, the preservation of the symmetric exchange axiom is checked directly, or equivalently interpreted using operations from discrete convex analysis: polarization is obtained by repeated splitting of variables and restricting to a Boolean slice, and projection is obtained by aggregation of variables; splitting and aggregation preserve M-convexity. For Lemma 3.27, introduce variables e_{ij} in R^{n^2} and maps φ(e_{ij})=e_i and ψ(e_{ij})=e_j. For a function ν, form φ^*ν and then push forward along ψ after adding a penalty ℓ_k with ℓ_k(e_{ij})=0 if i=j and k if i≠j. Explicitly ν_k=ψ_*(ℓ_k+φ^*ν). Splitting and aggregation preserve M-convexity, and the transportation/existence theorem for nonnegative matrices with prescribed row and column sums ensures ν_k is finite. As k grows, the penalty forces mass to remain on the diagonal exactly where possible, giving pointwise convergence to ν and the stated monotonicity outside dom(ν).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the real closed field of real convergent Puiseux series and let f_t be a homogeneous polynomial with coefficients in K_{≥0}. The following are equivalent over K: every finite product of nonnegative directional derivatives of f_t is either zero or log-concave on K^n_{>0}; every ordinary partial derivative ∂^α f_t is either zero or log-concave on K^n_{>0}; and f_t is Lorentzian in the Puiseux-series sense, namely it has M-convex support and all order d−2 derivatives are quadratic forms whose Hessians have at most one positive eigenvalue in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence is the transfer of Theorem 2.30 to the real closed field K. Proposition 2.33 expresses log-concavity of a homogeneous polynomial in terms of the inertia of its Hessian, and all relevant statements about signs of principal minors, nonnegativity of coefficients, and directional derivatives are first-order in the language of ordered fields once the coefficients are fixed. Since K is real closed and satisfies Tarski's principle, the real proof applies verbatim over K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.20 and Corollary 3.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the field of real convergent Puiseux series with valuation val, and let trop(f_t)(α)=val(s_α(t)) for f_t=Σ_{α in ∆^d_n}s_α(t)w^α with coefficients in K_{≥0}. For a function ν:∆^d_n→Q∪{∞}, ν is M-convex if and only if there exists a Lorentzian polynomial f_t in K[w_1,...,w_n] whose tropicalization is ν. More generally, tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over K are M-convex, and every real-valued M-convex function is a limit of tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over K. In particular, if M is a matroid with basis set B, the ordinary Dressian Dr(M) of valuated matroids with underlying matroid M satisfies Dr(M)=closure{−trop(f_t): f_t is Lorentzian over K and supp(f_t)=B}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the direction from Lorentzian tropicalization to M-convexity, it suffices to verify the local exchange axiom. Given α,β in dom(ν) with |α−β|_1=4, write them as γ+e_p+e_q and γ+e_r+e_s. The quadratic derivative ∂^γ f_t is Lorentzian, hence stable in the quadratic sense over K; Brändén's theorem that tropicalizations of homogeneous stable polynomials are M-convex gives the required local exchange inequality for ν. For the converse with rational ν, Theorem 3.14 says f^ν_q is Lorentzian for all sufficiently small positive real q. By quantifier elimination over real closed fields, the Puiseux polynomial f^ν_t=Σ t^{ν(α)}w^α/α! is Lorentzian over K, and its tropicalization is ν. Corollary 3.28 handles real-valued ν by approximating it by rational M-convex functions: after reducing to full domain using Lemma 3.27, polarize to a valuated matroid on a uniform matroid; the Dressian is a rational polyhedral fan, so rational points approximate; projecting back by Lemma 3.26 preserves M-convexity. The Dressian statement uses the convention that valuated matroids are M-concave, so a minus sign converts M-convex tropicalizations into valuated matroids.

proof_techniques.name: Encode M-convex functions as tropicalized Lorentzian polynomials
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that M-convex functions on ∆^d_n are precisely tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over Puiseux series, at least for rational-valued functions, and all real-valued M-convex functions are limits of such tropicalizations. The construction is explicit: for ν, use f^ν_t=Σ t^{ν(α)}w^α/α!, and Lorentzianity over the Puiseux field is certified by Theorem 3.14 plus Tarski transfer. Conversely, local quadratic derivatives and Brändén's tropicalization theorem for stable polynomials force the tropicalization to satisfy the local exchange axiom.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If elements of the relative Dressian can be represented by valuated matroids or M-convex functions satisfying extra incidence constraints with Trop(M), this technique suggests lifting them to Lorentzian polynomials, applying algebraic or linear operations there, and tropicalizing back.

proof_techniques.name: Nuij-type homotopy inside the Lorentzian cone
proof_techniques.description: The proof that L^d_n is the closure of strictly Lorentzian polynomials constructs an explicit homotopy using nonnegative averaging substitutions and repeated operators of the form 1+θ w_i∂_j. This homotopy deforms arbitrary Lorentzian polynomials toward the symmetric polynomial (w_1+...+w_n)^d while staying in the Lorentzian class after projectivization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proposed involution or extension map on a Dressian-like parameter space might be tested or constructed on dense/strict loci and then extended by closure. The homotopy also gives a canonical contraction of fixed support strata, useful if one needs to compare a desired map to its values on simpler matroidal boundary points such as flats.

proof_techniques.name: Support extraction from analytic inequalities
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly turns Hessian or Rayleigh inequalities into combinatorial exchange axioms. The key route is: Lorentzian Hessian signature implies c-Rayleigh inequalities; c-Rayleigh inequalities force interval convexity and then M-convexity of support; M-convex support is exactly the support condition for matroids or valuated matroids.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If containment of tropical linear spaces can be recast as inequalities or degenerations of coefficients, this technique may help show that the support of an image under a candidate duality operation remains a matroid or a permitted relative-Dressian object.

proof_techniques.name: Closure under nonnegative linear substitutions and derivatives
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.10 and Corollary 2.11 show that Lorentzian polynomials are stable under arbitrary nonnegative linear changes of variables and nonnegative directional derivatives. The proof decomposes substitutions into splitting, dilation, and diagonalization, all controlled by the fundamental operator 1+θ w_i∂_j.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Matroid operations such as deletion, contraction, restriction to flats, direct sums with loops, and certain tropical projections often correspond to specialization or differentiation at the level of generating polynomials. These closure properties are a ready-made mechanism for checking that such operations stay within the valuated-matroid/Lorentzian framework.

proof_techniques.name: Polarization and projection to move between polymatroids and matroids
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses polarization to replace general monomial exponents by multi-affine variables, and projection to return to the original variables. Both operations preserve Lorentzian polynomials, and their tropical/discrete analogues preserve M-convex functions. This allows many statements to be reduced to the Boolean slice, where matroid language applies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a relative Dressian construction involves non-multi-affine polymatroidal data or needs a reduction to ordinary matroids, polarization can transform the problem to a Boolean setting while preserving the relevant convexity/tropical structure.

proof_techniques.name: Linear-operator symbol criterion
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.2 gives a Lorentzian analogue of the Borcea-Brändén symbol method: a homogeneous linear operator preserves Lorentzian polynomials if its symbol is Lorentzian. The proof polarizes to the multi-affine case, multiplies by the input polynomial, applies pairwise Lorentzian-preserving merge operators, and specializes auxiliary variables.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A candidate order-reversing map on valuated objects might arise from a linear transformation on coefficient vectors, such as complementing bases, applying a Hodge-star-like operator, or summing over fibers. The symbol criterion provides a concrete way to verify that such an operator preserves the ambient Dressian/Lorentzian class.

proof_techniques.name: Coefficient-power deformation to canonical support polynomial
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.25 shows that raising all normalized coefficients of a Lorentzian polynomial to a power p∈[0,1] preserves Lorentzianity. Letting p go to 0 deforms any polynomial with fixed support J to the canonical exponential generating function f_J. This proves contractibility of each fixed M-convex support stratum.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the desired involution must extend a known map on flat-associated support objects, the deformation suggests reducing questions on a stratum to its support or unvaluated matroid. It may also help identify obstructions or canonical choices in fibers over supports.

proof_techniques.name: Local rank-two verification via tree metrics
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 3.14 reduces Lorentzianity of valuated objects to checking quadratic derivatives. In the Boolean full-support case, those quadratic derivatives are governed by rank-two valuated matroids, i.e. tree distances in the rank-two Dressian, and Lemma 3.21 translates tree-distance structure into a one-positive-eigenvalue condition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many tropical linear-space incidence conditions are local on rank-two contractions or links. This method suggests proving global extension properties by checking compatibility on rank-two minors, where the geometry is governed by trees.

proof_techniques.name: Real-closed-field transfer and tropical limiting
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically uses the real closed field of convergent Puiseux series and Tarski's quantifier elimination. Properties such as Lorentzianity, Hessian inertia, and log-concavity can be expressed first-order over ordered fields, so real q-deformation statements transfer to Puiseux polynomials whose valuations encode the desired tropical data.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a candidate map on a tropical or Dressian object, one can try to lift to Puiseux coefficients, prove an algebraic statement over K, and then tropicalize. This is particularly relevant when an involution is easier to define algebraically than purely piecewise-linearly.

proof_techniques.name: Contractibility of matroid-basis Lorentzian strata
proof_techniques.description: Combining Theorem 3.10 and Proposition 3.25, the paper shows that for every matroid basis set B, the projectivized space of multi-affine Lorentzian polynomials with support B is nonempty and contractible. This contrasts with stable polynomials, where some matroids, such as the Fano plane, have no stable polynomial with that support.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If relative Dressian strata are organized by underlying matroids or supports, contractibility of analogous Lorentzian strata may provide deformation-theoretic flexibility for extending maps from combinatorial boundary pieces to valuated interiors.

other_useful_info: The paper's notation Dr(M) refers to the ordinary Dressian of valuated matroids with fixed underlying matroid M, not to the relative Dressian of tropical linear subspaces inside Trop(M) from the original problem. The sign convention matters: tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials are M-convex, while valuated matroids are M-concave, so the paper identifies Dr(M) with the closure of negative tropicalizations. The paper cites Maclagan-Sturmfels for Dressians and Brändén 2010 for tropicalizations of stable polynomials as M-convex functions. It also records that every matroid basis support occurs for Lorentzian polynomials, unlike stable polynomials; for example, the Fano matroid has no stable polynomial with its basis support but does have a Lorentzian generating polynomial. Conjecture 2.29 says the full projectivized Lorentzian space P L^d_n should be a closed Euclidean ball; this is not proved but may be relevant if one looks for global topological obstructions to involutions.
why_relevant: Establishes the Lorentzian/M-convex framework underlying many recent results on valuated matroids and tropical incidence. Its convexity and tropicalization techniques feed directly into modern work on relative Dressians.
